pathophysiology_summary: Acute non-specific low back pain is mechanical pain from the muscles/ligaments/discs/facet joints without a specific serious cause, often triggered by a trivial movement, producing paraspinal muscle spasm and severe movement-limited pain that is nonetheless benign and self-limiting; strict bed rest is harmful and staying active speeds recovery. The overriding ground-truth task is red-flag screening: cauda equina syndrome (urinary retention/incontinence, faecal incontinence, saddle anaesthesia, bilateral/progressive leg weakness — a surgical emergency), fracture (significant trauma/osteoporosis, midline bony tenderness), infection (fever, IV drug use, immunosuppression), malignancy (history of cancer, weight loss, night pain, age >50), and significant radiculopathy. Here all red flags are negative, so it is benign mechanical back pain; imaging is NOT indicated, and management is reassurance, analgesia, a short course of a muscle relaxant if needed, heat, and early graded mobilisation.

differential.evidence_basis: Trivial-movement onset with severe paraspinal spasm and no neuro features.
Must exclude bladder/bowel/saddle/bilateral-leg features — a surgical emergency.
Screen trauma/osteoporosis, fever/IV drug use/immunosuppression, cancer/weight loss/night pain/age.
Radicular leg pain/neuro deficit would suggest this; absent here.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Mechanical back pain may lead, but cauda equina and the other serious causes must be actively excluded before conservative management.

differential.evidence_basis: No cauda equina features, no fracture/infection/malignancy red flags, no radiculopathy — benign mechanical back pain; no imaging.
No bladder/bowel/saddle/bilateral-leg features; escalation target if they develop.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative red-flag screen, benign mechanical back pain leads; analgesia + stay active (avoid strict bed rest), with a cauda equina safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; most improve over 2–6 weeks with active management; physiotherapy if slow.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign mechanical low back pain.

red_flags.description: Cauda equina syndrome — urinary retention/incontinence, faecal incontinence, saddle (perineal) anaesthesia, bilateral/progressive leg weakness or numbness
red_flags.clinical_significance: A surgical emergency needing urgent MRI and decompression.

red_flags.description: Serious-cause red flags — significant trauma/osteoporosis (fracture), fever/IV drug use/immunosuppression (infection), cancer history/weight loss/night pain/age>50 (malignancy)

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Ordering imaging for non-specific back pain
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: X-ray/MRI without red flags is not indicated and can promote unhelpful over-treatment.
Prolonged bed rest worsens outcomes; early graded activity is recommended.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Neurological examination (power, reflexes, sensation, straight-leg-raise), perianal tone
Physical transfer assistance off the floor
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reassurance + analgesia + short muscle relaxant if needed + heat + early graded mobilisation; 000/ED for cauda equina features; urgent review for fracture/infection/malignancy features. Arrange help to transfer safely.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing cauda equina or another serious cause.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP / Therapeutic Guidelines: Acute low back pain
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: For non-specific low back pain without red flags: reassure, avoid imaging, encourage staying active (avoid bed rest), simple analgesia (paracetamol/NSAID), consider a short course of a muscle relaxant. Screen and act on red flags (cauda equina, fracture, infection, malignancy).
